Event details

item.title: Figure/Ground: New Criteria
item.description:
Thursday, April 16, 2026 at 10 AM

Location: Henry Art Gallery, 15th Ave NE & 41st St, Seattle, WA

Figure/Ground: New Criteria is inspired by cultural historian Sarah Lewis’s concept of “groundwork aesthetics,” exploring figure-ground relationships and their connection to social tension. It builds on Lewis’s work, which—set against the expansion of Stand Your Ground laws across the United States since 2005, laws that broadly define rights to use lethal self-defense against perceived threats—describes a range of artistic interventions that reimagine “the literal and figurative meaning of ground.” Primarily featuring works from the Henry collection created in the twenty-first century, Figure/Ground reflects a period in which hard-won civil rights and claims to self-determination have been eroded across the US, disproportionately affecting Black, Brown, LGBTQ\+, and other marginalized communities.

From SEAtoday - Community & Civic

Event details

item.title: Beyond Mysticism: The Modern Northwest
item.description:
Thursday, April 16, 2026 at 10 AM

Location: Seattle Art Museum, 1300 First Avenue, Seattle, WA

image: Drift No. 2, 1936, Malcolm M. Roberts, American, 1913-1990, tempera on board, 25 1/2 x 20 5/8 in., Seattle Art Museum, Eugene Fuller Memorial Collection, 37.103, © Malcolm M. Roberts, photo: Scott Leen. Inspired by the region’s natural beauty and rapidly changing city, this exhibition showcases artists who told the raw story of Seattle in the 20th century. By sharing the Pacific Northwest’s perspective through trending art styles—including Social Realism, Surrealism, and Abstract Expressionism—these artists offered a unique take on Modernist art and challenged the “mystic” label ascribed to their works. Spearheading the movement were local icons Mark Tobey, Kenneth Callahan, Guy Anderson, and Morris Graves—but the range and breadth of Pacific Northwest art goes beyond the “Big Four” of the Northwest School. Featuring more than 150 pieces, this exhibition showcases works that investigate the complexities of daily life in the region. Whether applying an ecocritical lens or producing dreamy and uncanny visuals, these artists expressed the duality of the natural world and city life to warn of the environmental and social impacts of industrialization. From Malcolm Roberts to Kenjiro Nomura, learn how Northwest artists applied their regional perspective to broader artistic developments to create a new form of American Modernism. The exhibition is accompanied by a fully illustrated catalogue authored by Theresa Papanikolas, SAM’s Ann M. Barwick Curator of American Art.

From SEAtoday - Community & Civic

Event details

item.title: TADAIMA: I'm Home
item.description:
Thursday, April 16, 2026 at 10 AM

Location: Museum of History and Industry, 860 Terry Ave N, Seattle, Seattle, WA

After more than 80 years, a treasured collection returns “home” to Seattle’s Japanese American community. In 1942 dozens of traditional Boys’ and Girls’ Day dolls were entrusted to Bailey Gatzert Elementary School by Japanese American families facing forced incarceration. Most were never reclaimed after the war. Artist Miya Sukune uses recent research in MOHAI’s collection, as well as interviews with Japanese American survivors and their descendants, to create an installation called “Tadaima,” meaning “I’m home.” TADAIMA: “I’m Home!” Bringing Seattle’s Girls’ and Boys’ Day Dolls Home reconnects the dolls with their traditional role of protecting and blessing children. It invites visitors to celebrate the resilience, memory, and enduring traditions of the Japanese American Community.

item.title: Samantha Yun Wall: What We Leave Behind
item.description:
Thursday, April 16, 2026 at 10 AM

Location: Seattle Art Museum, 1300 First Avenue, Seattle, WA

Samantha Yun Wall’s new paintings use overlapping silhouettes of female figures as portals to unknown spaces and different temporal realms. Impetus for the new body of work is a Korean folk tale in which the Pasque flower is symbolic of a grandmother who passed away without the loving care of her grandchildren. It is a story of melancholy, loss, and remembrance. The delicate hair on the flower’s stem differentiates it, and the artist gives the plant a surreal aspect in some of her paintings, replacing the flower’s center with a watchful eye.

From SEAtoday - Community & Civic

Event details

item.title: Anila Quayyum Agha: Geometry of Light
item.description:
Thursday, April 16, 2026 at 10 AM

Location: Seattle Art Museum, 1300 First Avenue, Seattle, WA

Anila Quayyum Agha: Geometry of Light invites you to become part of the art. Agha, a Pakistani American artist, animates spaces with her large-scale sculptural installations. Suspended from the ceiling, Agha’s steel cubes are laser-cut with intricate designs that project geometric shadows onto the visitor. She draws on both the light and dark of her own life, using South Asian art practices to convey the gender discrimination she faced growing up as a young girl in Pakistan. An in-depth exploration of Islamic architecture, art, and identity, this is the first solo exhibition of a Pakistani American artist in SAM’s 90-year history. Agha is carving a name out for herself in the art world—visit the Seattle Asian Art Museum for an immersive and illuminating experience.

From SEAtoday - Community & Civic

Event details

item.title: Kameelah Janan Rasheed: we leak, we exceed
item.description:
Thursday, April 16, 2026 at 10 AM

Location: Henry Art Gallery, 15th Ave NE & 41st St, Seattle, WA

Kameelah Janan Rasheed \(b. 1985, East Palo Alto, CA; lives and works in Brooklyn, NY\) is a self-described “learner” who creates multi-media works that explore the poetics-pleasures-politics of Black knowledge production, information technologies, \[un\]learning, and belief formation. She often integrates found images and words alongside her own writing to create collaged wall installations and experimental video works. Across her work, Rasheed investigates the revision and life cycles of text, embracing expanded and embodied forms of writing and reading. we leak, we exceed will activate the unique volume and multiple vantage points of the Henry’s double-height gallery, drawing together threads from physics, Black critical thought, and information theory to create an immersive environment that interrogates the spatial and social implications of compression. A common process used in data storage, spatial organization, and information systems, compression abbreviates and collapses complex ideas into more simplified forms. Rasheed questions the way compression comes at the cost of nuance and creates unrecoverable losses. She draws parallels between the compression of information and the containment of people, both physically and through the structuring and defining of identities. Through a network of video, sound, and architectural mark-making, Rasheed proposes alternatively what she calls “an embrace of Black excess and expansion” as a liberatory practice.

item.title: Make a difference for cats and dogs in need!
item.description:
Friday, April 17, 2026 at 12 AM

Location: Pawsitive Alliance, 14150 NE 20th St. F1-233, Bellevue, WA

Are you interested in using your time, talent, and energy to support the vision of a healthy and happy home for every cat and dog in Washington? If yes, then please consider joining our Board! Pawsitive Alliance, a volunteer driven tax-exempt nonprofit organization, is having an open call for Board Members. Founded in 2005, the mission of Pawsitive Alliance is to help end the euthanasia of adoptable dogs and cats in Washington by increasing adoptions, supporting spay and neuter programs, and improving pet retention. Pawsitive Alliance advances are mission through our three programs: -  Adoption program: \#WhyNotMePets campaign, highlighting long-term homeless pets, and supporting adoption events to help homeless finds homes -  Spay/Neuter: providing vouchers and financial and logistical support to increase surgeries and access to end pet overpopulation and suffering -  Pet retention: collecting and distributing pet food and supplies and offering pet ID tags and microchips to keep pets thriving with their caretakers Our programs and activities prioritize increasing services for pets, people, and places across Washington state with few resources. Pawsitive Alliance’s biggest strengths are marketing, organizing, fundraising, and collaborating.  We work with more than 50 human and animal-welfare partners each year. Since 2005, we have helped over 8,000 animals find homes, delivered over 170,000 pounds of donated pet food, and facilitated more than 11,000 spay and neuter surgeries. The Pawsitive Alliance Board is a hands-on group of committed individuals, investing their time and resources to support our mission.  As we celebrate our 20th anniversary in 2025, and look to the future, we are looking to add individuals from diverse backgrounds to join the Board and help the organization’s continued success and growth.  Ideal candidates are committed to our mission, can commit at least 10-15 per month to the organization, are able to represent the organization in the community, are ready to engage in strategy setting, fundraising and networking, and bring a well-rounded set of skills to a unique and hard-working team.​ Pawsitive Alliance is always interested in bringing greater diversity and new areas of expertise, energy, and skills to the Board. For more information on the organization, refer to our website at www.pawsitivealliance.org.  Please fill out our Board Application if you share our passion for helping to end pet homelessness in Washington and are interested in serving and helping end pet overpopulation. Those interested in applying should email our Board Recruitment Committee at info@pawsitivealliance.org.

Event details

item.title: Springing Forward: Brunch for Black Economic Brilliance
item.description:
Friday, April 17, 2026 at 11 AM

Location: Amazon Frontier, 2205 7th Ave, Seattle, WA

Experience the Central District Community Preservation and Development Authority \(CDCPDA\) benefit brunch: Springing Forward: Brunch for Black Economic Brilliance. Now in its second year, this annual gathering brings together more than 200 visionaries—professionals, philanthropists, policymakers, and community leaders—united by a shared commitment to advancing Black economic empowerment in Seattle’s Central District and beyond.  Anchored in the themes: Collaborate. Sustain. Thrive. The brunch creates a dynamic space for meaningful connection, shared learning, and forward-looking action. The program will highlight current tenants, key partnerships, and community activations, while also providing important updates on the progress of the McKinney Center for Community and Economic Development—and a look ahead at what’s to come for the future of this vital project. Guests will engage directly with CDCPDA staff and board members, as well as tenants of the McKinney Center, a cornerstone community hub driving inclusive economic growth. In partnership with Amazon’s Black Employee Network, this event moves beyond celebration to the intentional cultivation of Black excellence and long-term community sustainability. Join us for a meaningful experience that goes beyond traditional networking—one rooted in collaboration, resilience, and collective progress. Your presence helps fuel transformative impact and strengthens the foundation for a thriving, equitable future. Attendee | $150.00﻿ ﻿Full access to the brunch, keynote, and networking. Your ticket directly supports the McKinney Center and community-rooted development in the Central District. Vendor Table | $175.00 Table your business or organization during the first hour of the event. Includes one attendee ticket. Full Table \(8 seats\) | $1,200.00 Bring your team or clients. Includes 8 individual tickets — a savings of $1,000 vs. individual pricing. Guest details can be submitted after purchase. Be part of the movement. Together, we spring forward—collaborating, sustaining, and thriving. Our Emcee TraeAnna Holiday, a true Seattleite, has watched her city change in many ways. She started training in media from the young age of 9 and continued through school up to Howard University and University of Washington. Through studying abroad multiple times, gaining her degree in Communications and Urban Studies, and being displaced by gentrification, she's now fueled to tell her neighborhood's stories through film and media. She found a home for this work at Converge Media as a two-time Emmy award winning Media Personality, TV Show Host and Producer, with her daily talk show, The Day With Trae, and other productions. As Communications Director for Washington state’s Office of Equity, she is able to bring her knowledge and enthusiasm for communications and the community to the state level. Flourishing with her style, passion, and drive, she dynamically creates impact media throughout her work, and helps the next generation find their voice in the world of media.  Our Keynote Speaker LaNesha McCoy is a skilled public historian with over 20 years experience leading African American museums and history initiatives. She is Executive Director of BlackPast.org, the world’s largest online encyclopedia on Black history which was founded by Dr. Quintard Taylor. Prior, she served as President & CEO of the Northwest African American Museum from 2017 to 2023 where she led strategic planning, recordsetting fundraising, and innovative programming. LaNesha is Past National President of the Association of African American Museums Board of Directors, only the sixth woman in history to serve in that capacity. A member of the International Council of Museums, she has studied museums globally in Kenya, Ghana, South Africa, England, Germany, and Israel. An ordained clergy, LaNesha is Assistant Pastor at the historic New Hope Missionary Baptist Church in Seattle's Central District and Executive Administrator of the New Hope Community Development Institute, which is focused on building affordable housing for people of color. Never forgetting her roots or the many mentors that shaped her life, she now has three master's degrees and is currently a PhD Candidate at the University of Washington where she teaches and researches African American educational history. She's a mentor and advocate, leading initiatives to support education.

Email Shameka.Gagnier@gmail.com or call/text \(720\) 212 7113 📞

Event details

item.title: Decay
item.description:
Friday, April 17, 2026 at 12 PM

Location: The Vestibule, 5919 15th Ave. N.W., Seattle, WA

Decay explores how we navigate and engage with the inevitable entropy of our world. This exhibition shows us the joy of degeneration, the beauty of collapse, the peace that comes from crumbling into dust. The work, primarily from Pacific Northwest artists, ranges from sculptural, to wall-mounted 3D, video and installation works. Included in this exhibition will be works created using natural and reclaimed materials, as well as pieces designed to create spaces for quiet reflection on the theme. Decay appears as part of the natural world and in our bodies in many forms, and so over the duration of the show, some works may change over time, exemplifying the theme. Many of the pieces prompt reflection on mortality, the fragility of the physical body and the sacred.

item.title: Scott Gibson - Visual Symphony II
item.description:
Friday, April 17, 2026 at 12 PM

Location: Gray Sky Gallery, 320 First Ave. S., Seattle, WA

In March, Visual Symphony II Scott Gibson invites viewers into a world where music and art collide. Each work is an exploration of the vivid, sensory experience of sound translating into vibrant, emotional visuals. As an artist with synesthesia each work expresses Scott’s personal perception of what music looks like. Scott uses resin techniques that he has pioneered and pushes the boundaries of this medium.

item.title: Forterra Fridays: April 2026
item.description:
Friday, April 17, 2026 at 1 PM

Location: Duwamish Hill Preserve, 3800 South 115th Street, Tukwila, WA

Forterra Fridays: April 2026 Friday, Apr. 17, 2026, 1:00 pm to 3:30 pm We’re excited to invite you to volunteer with us for a special Earth Month event at Duwamish Hill Preserve, a Tukwila park providing critical wildlife habitat and public trails in the middle of an industrial area. Join us on Friday, April 17th, from 1:00 pm to 3:30 pm, to restore essential wildlife habitat by removing Himalayan blackberries that threaten our local native trees, shrubs, and groundcover. Established native plants benefit local wildlife, improve water quality, and enhance air quality.  You will also gain valuable insights into ecological restoration and our innovative approach to land conservation. We’ll start with a walk of the site and Earth Day-themed trivia and have restoration professionals on hand to answer questions about plants, restoration, and the Green Tukwila Partnership and Forterra.  What we are doing: The Preserve now includes an outdoor classroom area, the Cultural Garden, an enhanced trail system, and continued restoration opportunities for volunteers. We’ll be removing the blackberry to ensure the trees, shrubs, and groundcover plants can thrive.   It’s a great opportunity to learn about Forterra’s innovative approach to land conservation, including its role in safeguarding the Duwamish Hill Preserve.  What to bring: Please dress for the weather \(in layers to stay warm/dry\). Snacks, drinks, tools, and gloves will all be provided. Where to park: Park in the nearby parking lot at Duwamish Hill Preserve. Signs direct you from the parking lot to where we will meet.  Date and Time: Friday, April 17, 2026, 1:00 –3:30 PM  Register and learn more here.

These monthly drop-in tours are included with Museum admission. When you arrive, please check-in with your ticket at the Admissions Desk. Tours meet outside of Osberg Great Hall, near the entrance to the Museum. Event details

item.title: Make a difference for cats and dogs in need!
item.description:
Saturday, April 18, 2026 at 12 AM

Location: Pawsitive Alliance, 14150 NE 20th St. F1-233, Bellevue, WA

Are you interested in using your time, talent, and energy to support the vision of a healthy and happy home for every cat and dog in Washington? If yes, then please consider joining our Board! Pawsitive Alliance, a volunteer driven tax-exempt nonprofit organization, is having an open call for Board Members. Founded in 2005, the mission of Pawsitive Alliance is to help end the euthanasia of adoptable dogs and cats in Washington by increasing adoptions, supporting spay and neuter programs, and improving pet retention. Pawsitive Alliance advances are mission through our three programs: -  Adoption program: \#WhyNotMePets campaign, highlighting long-term homeless pets, and supporting adoption events to help homeless finds homes -  Spay/Neuter: providing vouchers and financial and logistical support to increase surgeries and access to end pet overpopulation and suffering -  Pet retention: collecting and distributing pet food and supplies and offering pet ID tags and microchips to keep pets thriving with their caretakers Our programs and activities prioritize increasing services for pets, people, and places across Washington state with few resources. Pawsitive Alliance’s biggest strengths are marketing, organizing, fundraising, and collaborating.  We work with more than 50 human and animal-welfare partners each year. Since 2005, we have helped over 8,000 animals find homes, delivered over 170,000 pounds of donated pet food, and facilitated more than 11,000 spay and neuter surgeries. The Pawsitive Alliance Board is a hands-on group of committed individuals, investing their time and resources to support our mission.  As we celebrate our 20th anniversary in 2025, and look to the future, we are looking to add individuals from diverse backgrounds to join the Board and help the organization’s continued success and growth.  Ideal candidates are committed to our mission, can commit at least 10-15 per month to the organization, are able to represent the organization in the community, are ready to engage in strategy setting, fundraising and networking, and bring a well-rounded set of skills to a unique and hard-working team.​ Pawsitive Alliance is always interested in bringing greater diversity and new areas of expertise, energy, and skills to the Board. For more information on the organization, refer to our website at www.pawsitivealliance.org.  Please fill out our Board Application if you share our passion for helping to end pet homelessness in Washington and are interested in serving and helping end pet overpopulation. Those interested in applying should email our Board Recruitment Committee at info@pawsitivealliance.org.

item.title: Green Issaquah: Restoration Event at Park Pointe
item.description:
Saturday, April 18, 2026 at 9 AM

Location: SE Evans St, Issaquah, WA

Join the Issaquah Alps Trails Club and Green Issaquah on the third Saturday of each month for invasive species removal events at Park Pointe! We will be removing invasive species such as Himalayan blackberry bushes to prepare for our fall planting event. These invasive plants inhibit the growth of native plants, and removing them will ensure the longevity and health of our newest additions. This event is part of our ongoing efforts to help meet the Issaquah Climate Action Plan goal of 55% tree canopy cover by 2035. When and Where Date & Time: Saturday, April 18th, 2025, 9 AM - Noon Where: Park Pointe Where to Park Parking for Park Pointe is available along SE Evans Street, which is overflow for Issaquah High School and the parking for the Sportsman Club. Where to Meet We will be meeting at the end of the dirt road at the trailhead. What to Bring Bring your own gloves if you have them. There will be gloves available for those who don't have their own. Please dress in layers and in clothing you don't mind getting dirty. Closed-toed shoes are required. Long sleeves and pants, especially denim, are strongly recommended. Bring a full water bottle and snacks. Don't forget to bring your sunscreen! Point of Contact Anne Newcomb \(anne@annenewcomb.net\) and Matt LaBelle \(mattlabelle@hotmail.com\), your work party leaders.

Event details

item.title: Moomins' Sea Adventures & Tove and the Sea
item.description:
Saturday, April 18, 2026 at 10 AM

Location: National Nordic Museum, 2655 NW Market Street, Seattle, WA

Dive into the whimsical world of Moominvalley and the life of its creator, Tove Jansson, with *Moomins’ Sea Adventures* and *Tove and the Sea*. *Moomins’ Sea Adventures* brings Moominvalley to life with illustrations and life-sized characters from the stories. In the center of the exhibit is an interactive island complete with a lighthouse and rowboats that invites visitors to imagine adventures of their own. *Tove and the Sea* offers a more intimate counterpoint, tracing Tove Jansson’s lifelong relationship with the Finnish archipelago through large-scale photographs and reflective texts.

Event details

item.title: Figure/Ground: New Criteria
item.description:
Saturday, April 18, 2026 at 10 AM

Location: Henry Art Gallery, 15th Ave NE & 41st St, Seattle, WA

Figure/Ground: New Criteria is inspired by cultural historian Sarah Lewis’s concept of “groundwork aesthetics,” exploring figure-ground relationships and their connection to social tension. It builds on Lewis’s work, which—set against the expansion of Stand Your Ground laws across the United States since 2005, laws that broadly define rights to use lethal self-defense against perceived threats—describes a range of artistic interventions that reimagine “the literal and figurative meaning of ground.” Primarily featuring works from the Henry collection created in the twenty-first century, Figure/Ground reflects a period in which hard-won civil rights and claims to self-determination have been eroded across the US, disproportionately affecting Black, Brown, LGBTQ\+, and other marginalized communities.

From SEAtoday - Community & Civic

Event details

item.title: Beyond Mysticism: The Modern Northwest
item.description:
Saturday, April 18, 2026 at 10 AM

Location: Seattle Art Museum, 1300 First Avenue, Seattle, WA

image: Drift No. 2, 1936, Malcolm M. Roberts, American, 1913-1990, tempera on board, 25 1/2 x 20 5/8 in., Seattle Art Museum, Eugene Fuller Memorial Collection, 37.103, © Malcolm M. Roberts, photo: Scott Leen. Inspired by the region’s natural beauty and rapidly changing city, this exhibition showcases artists who told the raw story of Seattle in the 20th century. By sharing the Pacific Northwest’s perspective through trending art styles—including Social Realism, Surrealism, and Abstract Expressionism—these artists offered a unique take on Modernist art and challenged the “mystic” label ascribed to their works. Spearheading the movement were local icons Mark Tobey, Kenneth Callahan, Guy Anderson, and Morris Graves—but the range and breadth of Pacific Northwest art goes beyond the “Big Four” of the Northwest School. Featuring more than 150 pieces, this exhibition showcases works that investigate the complexities of daily life in the region. Whether applying an ecocritical lens or producing dreamy and uncanny visuals, these artists expressed the duality of the natural world and city life to warn of the environmental and social impacts of industrialization. From Malcolm Roberts to Kenjiro Nomura, learn how Northwest artists applied their regional perspective to broader artistic developments to create a new form of American Modernism. The exhibition is accompanied by a fully illustrated catalogue authored by Theresa Papanikolas, SAM’s Ann M. Barwick Curator of American Art.

Event details

item.title: TADAIMA: I'm Home
item.description:
Saturday, April 18, 2026 at 10 AM

Location: Museum of History and Industry, 860 Terry Ave N, Seattle, Seattle, WA

After more than 80 years, a treasured collection returns “home” to Seattle’s Japanese American community. In 1942 dozens of traditional Boys’ and Girls’ Day dolls were entrusted to Bailey Gatzert Elementary School by Japanese American families facing forced incarceration. Most were never reclaimed after the war. Artist Miya Sukune uses recent research in MOHAI’s collection, as well as interviews with Japanese American survivors and their descendants, to create an installation called “Tadaima,” meaning “I’m home.” TADAIMA: “I’m Home!” Bringing Seattle’s Girls’ and Boys’ Day Dolls Home reconnects the dolls with their traditional role of protecting and blessing children. It invites visitors to celebrate the resilience, memory, and enduring traditions of the Japanese American Community.

item.title: Samantha Yun Wall: What We Leave Behind
item.description:
Saturday, April 18, 2026 at 10 AM

Location: Seattle Art Museum, 1300 First Avenue, Seattle, WA

Samantha Yun Wall’s new paintings use overlapping silhouettes of female figures as portals to unknown spaces and different temporal realms. Impetus for the new body of work is a Korean folk tale in which the Pasque flower is symbolic of a grandmother who passed away without the loving care of her grandchildren. It is a story of melancholy, loss, and remembrance. The delicate hair on the flower’s stem differentiates it, and the artist gives the plant a surreal aspect in some of her paintings, replacing the flower’s center with a watchful eye.

item.title: Hiking Club to Mount Si 🌹
item.description:
Sunday, May 3, 2026 at 8 AM

We’re socialists who hike! May we’ll be hiking Mount Si (8.0 miles) in the North Bend area. Let’s enjoy some early season hiking. DSA membership not required. Meet in the Hilltop Red Apple parking lot. Snacks and transportation to the trail provided. Recommended pack list: * Hiking boots * Water * Lunch or snacks * Jacket * Map (Apple or Google offline download\, AllTrails) * Sunglasses (weather depending) * Rain coat (weather depending) * Phone battery pack (optional) See REI day hiking checklist for additional recommendations

item.title: Local Council Meeting
item.description:
Tuesday, May 5, 2026 at 7 PM

Location: Online (Zoom Call)\, WA

The Local Council (LC) is the elected leadership body which serves the members of Seattle DSA. During these short weekly check-ins\, LC members will primarily be ensuring work is on track\, though pressing business may occasionally be discussed and voted on. Members are welcome to observe LC meetings by RSVPing here. \nThe LC has an informal check-in meeting every Tuesday (except the fourth Tuesday of the month for the GMMs) from 7:00pm – 7:45pm over Zoom. \nIf there’s something specific you’d like to bring to the Local Council\, please fill out the LC Request Form which can be found on the private resources page of our website. \nIf you are a member that has not yet been added to our Slack (which is required to log in to the private resources page)\, you can request to be added here. \nPlease note that on rare occurrences\, LC meetings will be cancelled due to holidays or unusual circumstances and these changes may not always be reflected on this calendar — if you show up to the meeting and aren’t let in after 5 minutes\, that is almost certainly what has happened.

item.title: Socialist Educators Happy Hour
item.description:
Friday, May 8, 2026 at 6 PM

Location: Chuck’s Hop Shop (Central District)\, 2001 E Union St\, Seattle\, WA\, 98122\, United States

Socialist Educators Happy Hour\nEducators from all over (Seattle\, Renton\, Highline\, etc…) get together\, chat politics\, & share about struggles in their union. All union activists and socialists are welcome to attend! \nWe often anchor our discussions with a reading\, but we’ll provide a short summary so everyone can participate whether they read or not. \nWe meet 6pm every 2nd Friday of the month at Chuck’s Hop Shop in the Central District. Join us!

Event details

item.title: New Labor Organizer Training
item.description:
Saturday, May 9, 2026 at 2:30 PM

Location: Cathy Hildebrand Community Room\, 923 E John St\, Seattle\, Washington\, 98102\, United States

RSVP HERE Have you ever… Wanted more rights at work? Wanted to fight back against the corporate class? Been thinking “I need a union\,” but don’t know how to make it happen? This workshop will teach you what you need to get started at your workplace! This is intended for people at currently non-union workplaces who want to take the first steps toward organizing\, whether you want to join an existing union\, form a new one\, or organizing demands of your boss without a union at all. Our experienced labor organizers will teach you how to have an organizing conversation and how to form your Organizing Committee. When we organize\, we win! PLEASE NOTE: The Cathy Hillenbrand Community Room is NOT at Cal Anderson! Do not believe Google Maps! It is at the corner of 10th Ave E & John St. There will be a social to follow this event at Outer Planet Brewing!

From Seattle DSA

Event details

item.title: Build a Better Union panel discussion
item.description:
Saturday, May 9, 2026 at 2:30 PM

Location: Capitol Hill Branch Library\, 425 Harvard Ave E\, Seattle\, WA\, 98102\, United States

RSVP HERE In some unions\, it’s tough to get involved beyond paying dues. Maybe leadership is an exclusive club or maybe they’re just checked out\, but something needs to change. Even when you can get involved\, is it a struggle? Are you really being listened to? Are your coworkers engaged with the union\, or just paper members? Building democratic participation makes a stronger\, fighting union. Come hear from union members who are currently organizing to do just that\, even when union leadership doesn’t agree with them. Learn how these members are working to build a better union from the inside\, and how you can apply their lessions to your own local. If you are a current union staffer\, we ask that you not attend this event. Please join us afterwards at Outer Planet! The Cathy Hillenbrand Community Room is NOT at Cal Anderson! Do not believe Google Maps! It is at the corner of 10th Ave E & John St. There will be a social to follow this event at Outer Planet Brewing!

From Seattle DSA

Event details

item.title: May Labor Social
item.description:
Saturday, May 9, 2026 at 4 PM

Location: Outer Planet Brewing\, 1812 12th Avenue\, Seattle\, 98122\, United States

RSVP HERE \nParticipants in our two workshops today are invited to continue their discussion and share what they learned at this social just down the street at Outer Planet Brewing. Anyone is welcome whether you’re not a union member (yet)\, a long time union activist\, or anywhere in between. \nWe’ll also have members of our Unionize for Trans Rights campaign at the social to discuss their work! \nYou can register for the Build A Better Union panel (for current union members) or the New Organizer Training (for future union members)\, both immediately before this social!

From Seattle DSA

Saturdays in South Seattle: -Cooking shift: 1:30-4pm in Tukwila -Serving shift: 4:30-7pm at Cafe Red (across the street from the light rail station) To volunteer for a cooking or serving shift\, follow the instructions here. We are also looking for financial supporters. It costs just $2 to feed a hungry neighbor. Support here!

From Seattle DSA

Event details

item.title: Socialist Soccer! 2pm - 4pm
item.description:
Sunday, May 24, 2026 at 2 PM

Location: Brighton Playfield\, 6000 39th Ave S\, Seattle\, WA\, 98118\, United States

Come play soccer with fellow socialists! This is a fun\, enjoyable way to get outdoors\, exercise\, and meet friendly socialists as well as non-socialist soccer enthusiasts! All genders and skill levels are welcome! Safety is our top priority. To participate you must abide by our norms and agreements to avoid injuries and help us take care of one another. Join us at Brighton Playfield in South Seattle. Map: goo.gl/maps/c74cytjrRB1vpT7h7 We almost always play on the most southern edge of the field. We confirm if we have enough players each Sunday on a socialist soccer Signal chat. To be added\, download the Signal app\, and email your phone number to tdupuis42@gmail.com with a request to be added. The game is usually at 10 AM\, but that may change\, so make sure you are on the Socialist Soccer signal chat!

From Seattle DSA
